title: Energy levels in atoms and line spectra
summary: Paper 4 — Energy levels in atoms and line spectra (22.4). Premium notes with Senpai Corner diagrams and live animations.
learningObjectives: Describe the concept of discrete energy levels in atoms.
Explain electron transitions, photon absorption, and emission processes.
Relate atomic energy levels to the formation of line spectra and their significance.

simpleExplanation.title: Quantum Jumps: The Energy Ladder
simpleExplanation.summary: Electrons in atoms can't just have any energy; they are confined to specific 'steps' on an energy ladder. When they move between these steps, they either absorb or release exact packets of light energy called photons.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine climbing a ladder, but you can only stand on the rungs, not float in between them. Each rung represents a specific, allowed energy level for an electron. To move up, you need to gain a precise amount of energy; to move down, you release a precise amount of energy.
steps: Electrons exist only in discrete energy levels (like ladder rungs).
Gaining specific energy (e.g., from a photon) moves an electron to a higher, 'excited' level.
An electron spontaneously drops to a lower level, emitting a photon with precise energy.
These emitted (or absorbed) photons create unique 'fingerprint' line spectra for each element.

flashcards.front: What does 'quantisation of energy levels' mean?
flashcards.back: Electrons in an atom can only occupy specific, discrete energy values, not any value in between.

flashcards.front: Define the 'ground state' of an electron.
flashcards.back: The lowest and most stable energy level an electron can occupy within an atom.
It absorbs a photon (or gains energy from a collision) whose energy exactly matches the difference between its current level and a higher energy level.

flashcards.front: What happens when an excited electron 'de-excites'?
flashcards.back: It transitions to a lower, more stable energy level, emitting a photon with energy precisely equal to the energy difference between the two levels.

flashcards.front: State the formula for photon energy during an electron transition.
flashcards.back: $E_{photon} = hf = E_{initial} - E_{final}$ (where $E_{initial} > E_{final}$ for emission).
A fundamental constant linking the energy of a photon to its frequency: $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$.

flashcards.front: What is an electronvolt (eV) and its conversion to Joules?
flashcards.back: An electronvolt is the kinetic energy gained by an electron accelerated through a potential difference of 1 volt. $1 \text{ eV} = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.
A spectrum showing discrete bright lines against a dark background, formed by photons emitted during electron de-excitation in excited atoms.
A spectrum showing dark lines at specific wavelengths against a continuous background, formed when atoms absorb photons, causing electrons to excite.

flashcards.front: Why is a line spectrum considered an atomic 'fingerprint'?
flashcards.back: Each element has a unique set of discrete energy levels, leading to a distinct pattern of photon energies that can be absorbed or emitted, identifying the element.

sections.content: Imagine an electron as an elevator that can only stop on specific floors, never in between. This is the essence of **quantised energy levels** within atoms. These discrete energy steps dictate how electrons absorb and emit energy, leading to the fascinating phenomenon of **line spectra** – the unique 'fingerprints' of elements. This lesson aligns with **Senpai Corner** A Level notes (2025–2027) for topic **22.4**.

Electrons orbiting an atom's nucleus are not free to possess any amount of energy. Instead, they are restricted to specific, fixed energy states. This fundamental concept is known as **energy quantisation**. These allowed states are typically visualised using an **energy-level diagram**, where the lowest and most stable energy state is called the **ground state**. Higher, less stable states are known as **excited states**.

items: 22.4 Energy levels in atoms and line spectra.
Energy levels (also called electron shells) are fixed distances from the nucleus of an atom which electrons can be found.
You can stand on one step or another but not in-between.
Electrons are the same thing; they can occupy one energy level or another but not the space in-between energy levels.
Electrons usually occupy the lowest energy level available (ground state).

sections.content: For an electron to move from a lower energy level to a higher one (an **excitation**), it must absorb a precise amount of energy. This energy is most commonly provided by an incident **photon** whose energy exactly matches the difference between the two levels. Since excited states are unstable, electrons quickly 'fall' back to lower, more stable levels (a **de-excitation** process). During this transition, the electron releases its excess energy by emitting a photon.

Here, $h$ is **Planck's constant** ($6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}$), $f$ is the frequency of the emitted photon, $E_{initial}$ is the higher energy level, and $E_{final}$ is the lower energy level. Crucially, the energy of the photon is *exactly* the difference between the initial and final electron energy levels. This precise energy difference dictates the photon's frequency and wavelength.

items: Excitation: Electron absorbs a photon, jumps to a higher energy level.
De-excitation: Electron drops to a lower energy level, emits a photon.
Photon energy equals the exact energy difference between levels.
Planck's constant links photon energy to its frequency.

sections.content: The Electronvolt (eV): A Convenient Energy Unit

When dealing with atomic energies, Joules ($J$) are often inconveniently small. Physicists frequently use the **electronvolt (eV)**, a more practical unit. One electronvolt is defined as the kinetic energy gained by a single electron when it is accelerated through an electrical potential difference of one volt.

items: Electronvolt (eV) is a common unit for atomic energies.
It represents energy gained by an electron accelerating through 1 volt.
Essential to convert between eV and J for calculations.

sections.question: An electron in a hydrogen atom de-excites from an energy level of -1.51 eV to a ground state level of -13.6 eV. Calculate the frequency of the emitted photon.
sections.solution:
1. **Find the energy difference (in eV):**
   $ \Delta E = E_{initial} - E_{final} = -1.51 \text{ eV} - (-13.6 \text{ eV}) = 12.09 \text{ eV}$ 

2. **Convert the energy difference to Joules:**
   $ \Delta E = 12.09 \text{ eV} \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}) = 1.9344 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}$ 

3. **Use the formula $E = hf$ to find the frequency ($f$):**
   $ f = \frac{\Delta E}{h} = \frac{1.9344 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ Js}} $ 

4. **Calculate the frequency:**
   $ f \approx 2.92 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$ 

   The emitted photon has a frequency of approximately $2.92 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$.

sections.content: When atoms are excited and then de-excite, they emit photons of specific, discrete energies. Collecting and separating these photons by wavelength produces an **emission spectrum** – a unique pattern of bright lines against a dark background. Each line corresponds to a specific electron transition. This pattern is unique to each element, like a barcode.

Conversely, an **absorption spectrum** is formed when a continuous spectrum of light passes through a cool gas. Atoms in the gas absorb photons with energies matching their electron's excitation energies, creating dark lines at specific wavelengths within the otherwise continuous spectrum. For any given element, the dark lines in its absorption spectrum precisely match the bright lines in its emission spectrum.

items: Emission spectrum: Bright lines from emitted photons during de-excitation.
Absorption spectrum: Dark lines from absorbed photons during excitation.
Each element's line spectrum is unique – its atomic 'fingerprint'.
Spectra are crucial for identifying elements in unknown samples (e.g., stars).

sections.content: Remember to convert electronvolts (eV) to Joules (J) when using Planck's constant in calculations, as Planck's constant is in Joules-seconds (Js). Pay close attention to negative signs when calculating energy differences between levels!

items.label: Senpai Corner A Level Physics notes (source)

faq.q: Why do atoms have discrete energy levels instead of continuous ones?
faq.a: Quantum mechanics dictates that electrons are confined within specific orbitals around the nucleus. These confined states lead to only certain, discrete energy values being allowed, a phenomenon called quantisation.

faq.q: How can line spectra be used to identify elements?
faq.a: Each element possesses a unique set of electron energy levels. This means it absorbs and emits photons of very specific energies, creating a distinct pattern of lines in its spectrum that acts as a unique 'fingerprint', allowing for elemental identification.

faq.q: What's the main difference between an emission and an absorption spectrum?
faq.a: An emission spectrum shows bright lines (photons emitted by excited atoms), while an absorption spectrum shows dark lines (photons absorbed by atoms) against a continuous background. Both sets of lines occur at the exact same wavelengths for a given element.
